beats.text: In Warmup, all four scales worked for your data. You had options!
But what happens when the numbers are bigger? Let's find out.

params.description: Data table appears. Books Read This Month. Aisha: 20, Ben: 35, Carlos: 55, Dana: 80.

beats.text: Here's data about how many books four students read this month. Dana read the most—80 books!

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph. Axis 0-50. Scale of 5 selected. Graph empty.

beats.text: Let me try Scale of 5 and see what happens.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.

beats.text: Hmm. Carlos's bar is just above my graph, but Dana's bar goes past the top. The graph only goes up to 50 right now.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph. Aisha, Ben, Carlos bars visible. Dana's bar extends past top. Warning indicator present.

beats.text: I COULD make the graph taller to fit 80. Let's see... I'd need 55, 60, 65, 70, 75, 80...

params.description: Axis extends upward. Tick marks appear: 55, 60, 65, 70, 75, 80. Axis now crowded with many tick marks.
Axis now extends from 0 to 80 with scale of 5.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph with extended axis 0-80. All tick marks visible (0, 5, 10...75, 80). Axis appears crowded.

beats.text: That's a LOT of numbers on the axis. 0, 5, 10, 15, 20... all the way up to 80. That's 17 tick marks!

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.

beats.text: It works... but is there a better way?

beats.text: When one scale needs too many tick marks, try a bigger scale.

beats.text: Scale of 5 works, but that's a lot of tick marks. Let's try a bigger scale.
Click Scale of 10 to see what happens.

validator.description: Student clicked Scale of 10 button

params.description: Graph updates to scale of 10. Axis now shows 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80—only 9 tick marks. All four bars fit cleanly. Checkmark indicator appears.

beats.text: Look at that—only 9 tick marks now instead of 17. Much cleaner!

beats.text: Here's the first thing to check: What's your BIGGEST number? That tells you what scales might work.
What's the biggest number in this data?

validator.description: Student selected 80, correct

beats.text: 80 is the biggest. Always check the biggest number first—does it fit on the scale without too many lines on the axis?

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values. Dana's 80 highlighted.
Vertical bar graph showing Books Read This Month data with scale of 10, axis 0–80, 9 tick marks.

beats.text: You saw that Scale of 5 needed 17 tick marks. Scale of 10 only needs 9. For big data, bigger scales make cleaner graphs.
When more than one scale fits, pick the one that's easier to read.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values. Dana's 80 highlighted.

params.description: Horizontal data table appears showing Marbles in Jars data: Jar A: 7, Jar B: 12, Jar C: 19, Jar D: 23.

beats.text: Here's data about marbles. Look at the biggest number—23. That's pretty small!

elements.description: Horizontal data table showing Marbles in Jars data: Jar A: 7, Jar B: 12, Jar C: 19, Jar D: 23.
Scale selector panel with four scale buttons visible. No bars displayed yet.

beats.text: This graph is horizontal—the bars go sideways. Let's check: which scales fit? Try clicking each scale.
Click each scale to see if 23 fits.

beats.text: You checked them all. All four scales fit!

elements.description: Horizontal data table showing Marbles in Jars data: Jar A: 7, Jar B: 12, Jar C: 19, Jar D: 23.

beats.text: All four scales fit! Let's look at Scale of 1.
Click Scale of 1 to see what it looks like.

validator.description: Student clicked Scale of 1 button

params.description: Horizontal bar graph displays with scale of 1. All four bars fit correctly, but axis shows 24 tick marks (0 through 23).

beats.text: It works—every number lands exactly. But look at all those lines on the side!

elements.description: Horizontal data table showing Marbles in Jars data: Jar A: 7, Jar B: 12, Jar C: 19, Jar D: 23.

params.description: Data table appears showing Marbles in Jars data: Jar A=7, Jar B=12, Jar C=19, Jar D=23.
Horizontal bar graph appears. Marbles in Jars data. Scale of 1, axis 0–23 with 24 tick marks visible.

beats.text: Scale of 1 works, but that's a lot of lines. You learned bigger scales are usually easier to read.

elements.description: Data table showing Marbles in Jars values: Jar A=7, Jar B=12, Jar C=19, Jar D=23.
Horizontal bar graph. Marbles in Jars. Scale of 1, axis 0–23, 24 tick marks. Four bars displayed.

beats.text: Let's try Scale of 10.
Click 'Scale of 10' to compare.

validator.description: Student clicked Scale of 10

params.description: Graph updates to scale of 10. Bars appear with ⚠️ indicators on Jar A and Jar C. Jar A (7) between 0 and 10. Jar C (19) between 10 and 20. Axis shows 0, 10, 20, 30—3 tick marks.

elements.description: Data table showing Marbles in Jars values: Jar A=7, Jar B=12, Jar C=19, Jar D=23.
Horizontal bar graph. Marbles in Jars. Scale of 10, axis 0–30, 4 tick marks. ⚠️ indicators on Jar A and Jar C bars.

beats.text: Hmm. Scale of 10 fits, but there are only 3 marks on the axis. Look at those warnings. Can you tell the exact value of where those bars end? Someone looking at our graph without the data table would need to guess how many marbles are shown.

params.description: ⚠️ on Jar A highlights with message: '7 won't land exactly on a line or in the middle.'

elements.description: Data table showing Marbles in Jars values: Jar A=7, Jar B=12, Jar C=19, Jar D=23.
Horizontal bar graph. Marbles in Jars. Scale of 10, axis 0–30. ⚠️ on Jar A showing exactness warning message.

beats.text: For big data, we'd pick Scale of 10 anyway. It's the easiest to read because we don't want too many lines on the axis. But these numbers are small and it's hard to find the exact location of the bar on the graph. We can do better.
When data is small, you have room to find a scale where everything lands exactly.

elements.description: Data table showing Marbles in Jars values: Jar A=7, Jar B=12, Jar C=19, Jar D=23.
Horizontal bar graph. Marbles in Jars. Scale of 10, axis 0–30, 4 tick marks. ⚠️ indicators on Jar A and Jar C.

params.description: Data table appears. Marbles in Jars. Jar A=7, Jar B=12, Jar C=19, Jar D=23.
Horizontal bar graph appears. Marbles in Jars data. Scale of 10. Axis 0–30. Warning indicators on Jar A and Jar C bars (values 7 and 19 do not land exactly on tick marks or midpoints).

beats.text: Let's try a smaller scale. Try a Scale of 2.
Click 'Scale of 2' to preview.

validator.description: Student clicked Scale of 2 button. Preview displays: all bars land exactly, checkmark indicator, no warnings.

params.description: Graph updates to Scale of 2. Axis 0–24 with 13 tick marks. All bars land exactly. Checkmark indicator appears. No warnings.

beats.text: Look—we can read the end of the bars clearly. ALL the numbers land exactly. 7 is right at 7, exactly halfway between 6 and 8. 12 is right at 12.

elements.description: Data table showing Marbles in Jars. Jar A=7, Jar B=12, Jar C=19, Jar D=23.
Horizontal bar graph. Marbles in Jars. Scale of 2. Axis 0–24. All bars land exactly. Checkmark indicator visible. No warnings.
Data table showing Marbles in Jars. Jar A=7, Jar B=12, Jar C=19, Jar D=23.
Horizontal bar graph. Marbles in Jars. Scale of 10 active. Axis 0–30. Warning indicators on Jar A and Jar C. Scale selector interactive.

beats.text: With Scale of 2, half of 2 is 1. So you can show ANY whole number exactly.
Scale of 2 works to show the exact value for all numbers—and for small data, the graph won't have too many marks on the axis.

elements.description: Data table showing Marbles in Jars. Jar A=7, Jar B=12, Jar C=19, Jar D=23.
Horizontal bar graph. Marbles in Jars. Scale of 2. Axis 0–24. All bars land exactly. Checkmark indicator visible.

params.description: Left panel labeled 'Data Set A' shows four values: 20, 35, 55, 80 with annotation 'Ones digits: 0 or 5'.

beats.flag: placeholder — image toy spec not yet defined; using as static visual display

params.description: Right panel labeled 'Data Set B' shows four values: 7, 12, 19, 23 with annotation 'Ones digits: 7, 2, 9, 3'.

beats.flag: placeholder — image toy spec not yet defined; using as static visual display

beats.text: Here's a quick way to know if you need Scale of 2. Check the last digit of each number in the ones place.

elements.description: Left panel: Data Set A showing 20, 35, 55, 80 labeled 'Ones digits: 0 or 5'.
Right panel: Data Set B showing 7, 12, 19, 23 labeled 'Ones digits: 7, 2, 9, 3'.

beats.text: Numbers that end in 0 or 5—like 20, 35, 55, 80—those are multiples of 5 and can be shown clearly with Scales of 5 and 10.

elements.description: Left panel: Data Set A showing 20, 35, 55, 80 labeled 'Ones digits: 0 or 5'.
Right panel: Data Set B showing 7, 12, 19, 23 labeled 'Ones digits: 7, 2, 9, 3'.

beats.text: But look at 7, 12, 19, 23. See the digits in the ones place? 7, 2, 9, 3. None are 0 or 5—that tells you they aren't multiples of 5 so Scale of 2 might be your best choice.
Check our groups. Which data set has last digits that are NOT 0 or 5?
Which data set has last digits that are NOT 0 or 5?

validator.description: Student clicked Data Set B (7, 12, 19, 23), correct

params.description: Data Set B panel highlights to confirm selection.

beats.text: Right. Look at those last digits: 7, 2, 9, 3. None are 0 or 5—that's your Scale of 2 signal.

elements.description: Left panel: Data Set A showing 20, 35, 55, 80 labeled 'Ones digits: 0 or 5'.
Right panel: Data Set B showing 7, 12, 19, 23 labeled 'Ones digits: 7, 2, 9, 3'. Click target active.

params.description: Vertical data table appears. Points Scored. Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31.

beats.text: Here's data from a game. Four rounds of points: 22, 15, 8, 31.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Points Scored. Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31.

beats.text: Check the ones place digits: 2, 5, 8, 1. Do they all end in 0 or 5?
22... no. 15... yes! 8... no. 31... no. Three of them don't so they aren't multiples of 5. Which scale will show ALL values exactly?
Which scale will show ALL values exactly?

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 2, correct

params.description: Vertical bar graph appears. Points Scored. Scale of 2. Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31. All bars land exactly on tick marks.

beats.text: Scale of 2. All of the bars fit well on the graph and their exact values are clear.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Points Scored. Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31.

Vertical data table showing Points Scored. Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31.

params.description: Bar graph updates to Scale of 1. Same Points Scored data.

beats.text: Scale of 1 also shows the exact values, but it would require a lot of marks on the axis to get to the greatest value of 31. Let's consider when we might choose a scale of 1.

beats.text: Here's data about students' favorite fruits. The most votes went to Dates with 60. Scale of 1 doesn't make sense here because it would take 60 lines on the axis. That's too much!
Your job: pick the right scale. Which scale will you use?
Explore the Scale Preview System with scales 2, 5, and 10.

beats.text: Why is Scale of 10 a good choice? Choose all of the reasons.
Why is Scale of 10 a good choice? Choose all of the reasons.
options: All values end in 0 or 5 so they can be graphed precisely.
The graph is easy to read.
The biggest number is 60 so the bars fit on the graph.
Scale of 2 takes too many marks to reach the biggest number.

and.selected: All values end in 0 or 5 so they can be graphed precisely.
The graph is easy to read.
The biggest number is 60 so the bars fit on the graph.
Scale of 2 takes too many marks to reach the biggest number.

validator.description: Student selected all four reasons, correct

beats.text: Exactly. All values are multiples of 5, so scales of 5 and 10 both work to read the graph clearly. For data going up to 60, Scale of 10 gives you a cleaner graph.

validator.description: Student did not select all four reasons

beats.text: Let's consider the questions we ask when choosing a scale. What is the biggest number? Does the data all fit? Look at all the numbers—do they end in 0 or 5 so we can find the exact location of the bars? What scale can we use for the best looking graph? All of these reasons help us choose the scale of 10.

beats.text: These numbers aren't multiples of 5 so the values can't be shown exactly with that scale. Try scale of 2.

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 1

beats.text: Scale of 1 lets us graph exact numbers, but the largest value is 41. That would be a lot of marks on the axis. Which scale would be exact and look cleaner?

beats.text: Let's see what you know.
